files.note:
NEW FILE (I own this). The whole x86_64 M4 ring0<->ring3 round-trip. Public surface: `pub fn user_demo() -> bool`. Design: map a fresh ring3-accessible code+stack page (U/S=1 at PML4E/PDPTE/PDE/PTE; placed in vacant PML4[1] = VA 0x80_0000_0000, NOT boot PML4[0] which is U/S=0) spliced into live CR3; copy the PIC stub (`mov rax,0xCAFE; int 0x80; jmp .`) into an isolated .bss code frame; `enter_ring3` parks kernel callee-saved + the resume SP, builds the iret frame [SS,RSP,RFLAGS,CS,RIP] and `iretq`s to ring3; the stub's `int 0x80` traps through a DPL=3 gate to `syscall_entry` on TSS.RSP0; the handler records arg + prints `syscall from user: arg=0x...cafe`, then `syscall_entry` does a non-local return (reload parked SP, pop callee-saved, `ret`) landing back in user_demo. Returns true iff arg 0xCAFE was observed from ring3.

VERIFIED: IRET pop order RIP,CS,RFLAGS,RSP,SS confirmed against Intel SDM Vol.2 IRET 'Operation' pseudocode (IA-32e 64-bit + RETURN-TO-OUTER-PRIVILEGE-LEVEL) via felixcloutier; U/S bit at every level = SDM Vol.3A 4.6; TSS.RSP0 at byte 0x04 = SDM Vol.3A 7.7 Fig 7-11 + 6.14.4; gate DPL>=CPL for `int n` = SDM 6.12.1.2; user access bytes 0xFA/0xF2 = SDM 3.4.5 Fig 3-8.

COMPILE-VERIFIED: `cargo check -p tb-hal` (host x86_64) passes with the full call graph live (0 errors); the `naked_asm!` bodies (enter_ring3/syscall_entry) and the `global_asm!` stub both codegen+assemble cleanly in isolation on host. (The ELF `.section ...,@progbits` directive only assembles on the real x86_64-tabos-none ELF target, exactly like the existing trap.rs/boot.rs; the Windows-COFF host rejects that directive for all of them, which is expected and not a defect.)

DELIBERATE CHOICE: the stub is COPIED into an isolated frame rather than aliasing the kernel .text page into ring3 (avoids exposing kernel code to ring3 + any page straddle); the stub bytes are still sourced via its identity-mapped symbol.

CROSS-CUTTING WIRING REQUIRED (shared files NOT emitted here to avoid clobbering the aarch64 agent's concurrent edits — apply once at integration): (1) crates/tb-hal/src/arch/mod.rs: add `user_demo` to the `pub use self::x86_64::{...}` list (and the aarch64 list when that arm lands). (2) crates/tb-hal/src/lib.rs: add `pub fn user_demo() -> bool { arch::user_demo() }` with a doc comment (no other change). (3) kernel/src/main.rs: keep M0-M3 lines verbatim; replace the final `tb_hal::halt()` after the M3 block with: `tb_hal::serial_write_str("user-test: entering unprivileged mode\n"); if tb_hal::user_demo() { tb_hal::serial_write_str("M4: user/ring OK\n") } else { tb_hal::serial_write_str("M4: FAIL\n") } tb_hal::halt()`. (4) scripts/run-x86_64.sh: change `MARKER='M3: mmu OK'` to `MARKER='M4: user/ring OK'`. I verified the wiring compiles by applying (1)+(2) temporarily, then reverted both shared files to their original hashes.

UPDATED (I own this). Two additive changes, all M0-M3 exports/behavior preserved verbatim: (1) added `pub mod user;` (the new M4 module); (2) added `pub use user::user_demo;` so the shared arch/mod.rs can re-export it (arch/mod.rs wiring is cross-cutting; see user.rs note). Also extended the module doc with an M4 paragraph. install_traps()/halt()/all M1-M3 re-exports unchanged.

UPDATED (I own this). M4 additions, all M0-M3 GDT/TSS/IST behavior preserved: (1) USER_CODE_SEL=0x28 (GDT idx5) / USER_DATA_SEL=0x30 (idx6), pub(super) so user.rs ORs `|3` for ring3; (2) USER_CODE_DESC=0x00AF_FA00_0000_FFFF (access 0xFA: P,DPL3,S,exec,RW; G,L) and USER_DATA_DESC=0x00CF_F200_0000_FFFF (access 0xF2: P,DPL3,S,RW; G,DB) per Intel SDM Vol.3A 3.4.5 Fig 3-8; (3) GDT grown from [u64;5] to [u64;7] (gdtr limit auto-tracks via size_of); init() now writes entries 5 and 6; (4) TSS_RSP0 dword index (byte 0x04) + a dedicated 16 KiB 16-aligned RSP0_STACK; init() programs TSS.RSP0 to its top BEFORE any ring3 entry (SDM 7.7 Fig 7-11 / 6.14.4); (5) `set_rsp0(u64)` helper (per spec) kept #[allow(dead_code)] since init()'s default suffices. The M1 IST setup, tss_descriptor, and load_gdt_and_segments are byte-for-byte unchanged. Verified: tb-hal `cargo check` (host x86_64) passes.

UPDATED (I own this). M4 additions, all M1 IDT behavior preserved: (1) IdtEntry::set_user(handler) — writes a DPL=3 64-bit interrupt gate (type_attr 0xEE) on the ring0 code selector, no IST, so ring3's `int 0x80` is permitted (Intel SDM Vol.3A 6.12.1.2 CPL<=gate DPL; Fig 6-8); (2) pub(super) fn set_user_gate(vector, handler) — rewrites a single gate in the already-loaded IDT in place (no lidt reload needed; CPU re-reads the IDT per interrupt). The original IdtEntry::set (0x8E), the 256-gate init(), and the IST routing for #DF/NMI/#MC are unchanged. user_demo() calls set_user_gate(0x80, syscall_entry). Verified: tb-hal `cargo check` (host x86_64) passes.

NEW FILE (I own this). The whole aarch64 M4 round-trip: maps a user code + stack page under L1[3] via the live TTBR0 root (no dependency on mmu.rs internals), drops to EL0 via eret with SPSR=EL0t, the user stub issues `svc #0` with x0=0xCAFE, the SVC handler records it and longjmps back into the kernel. Exposes `pub fn user_demo() -> bool`. INTEGRATION REQUIRED in files I do NOT own: (1) crates/tb-hal/src/arch/aarch64/mod.rs — add `mod user;` + `pub use user::user_demo;` (provided in my mod.rs deliverable); (2) crates/tb-hal/src/arch/mod.rs — add `user_demo` to BOTH `#[cfg(target_arch="x86_64")] pub use self::x86_64::{...}` and `#[cfg(target_arch="aarch64")] pub use self::aarch64::{...}` re-export lists; (3) crates/tb-hal/src/lib.rs — add `/// ... pub fn user_demo() -> bool { arch::user_demo() }`; (4) kernel/src/main.rs — after the M3 block, before halt: `tb_hal::serial_write_str("user-test: entering unprivileged mode
"); if tb_hal::user_demo() { tb_hal::serial_write_str("M4: user/ring OK
") } else { tb_hal::serial_write_str("M4: FAIL
") }`; (5) scripts/run-aarch64.sh — change MARKER to "M4: user/ring OK". The x86_64 owner provides the x86 user.rs + the same shared-file wiring for the x86 arm; the shared files must union both arches' user_demo.

UPDATED (I own this). Adds `mod user;` and `pub use user::user_demo;` so the arch dispatch (crates/tb-hal/src/arch/mod.rs) can re-export `user_demo` for aarch64. install_traps()/halt() and ALL M0-M3 behavior are byte-for-byte preserved; only the module list, the re-export block, and the doc comment changed.

UPDATED (I own this). ONLY two functional changes vs M3: (1) vector slot 8 ('Lower EL using AArch64, Synchronous', offset 0x400) now routes to the real trampoline `__vec_el0_sync` instead of `__vec_other`; (2) the new `__vec_el0_sync` trampoline does SAVE_CONTEXT, passes &Frame to `aarch64_el0_sync_handler` (in user.rs) via `bl`, and parks defensively (the handler is `-> !` and longjmps to the kernel, never returning here). The M1 Current-EL-SPx Sync path (brk), `__vec_other`, `__vec_sync`, `__trap_dispatch`, SAVE_CONTEXT/RESTORE_CONTEXT and the 16x128B table layout are byte-for-byte preserved. SAVE_CONTEXT keeps SP 16-aligned (0x110 % 16 == 0); on EL0->EL1h entry SP=SP_EL1 (kernel stack), so the frame lands on the kernel stack as required.

OWNED. Preserves the entire M0/M1/M2/M3 API verbatim (signatures/types/semantics) and ADDS pub fn user_demo()->bool delegating to arch::user_demo, plus an M4 module-doc bullet. Comment prose condensed only where non-load-bearing; all items keep docs (#![deny(missing_docs)]).

OWNED. M0/M1/M2/M3 sequence kept verbatim (hello -> trap round-trip -> ping-pong-1000+canary -> mmu_selftest, with the exact markers). M3 failure now halts before M4 (fail-closed). Appends the M4 steps EXACTLY as the milestone spec requires: 'user-test: entering unprivileged mode' then user_demo() -> 'M4: user/ring OK' or 'M4: FAIL', then halt(). Crate stays free of unsafe {} blocks.

OWNED. MARKER changed from 'M3: mmu OK' to 'M4: user/ring OK'. Header comments updated to reflect M4 as the DoD; everything else (microvm/PVH launch, fail-closed timeout, KVM-vs-TCG selection) unchanged.
files.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/run-x86_64.sh - QEMU launcher + Definition-of-Done check for M4 (x86_64).
#
# Boots the PVH ELF kernel under QEMU 'microvm' (the machine type Firecracker is
# modelled on), wires legacy 16550 COM1 to stdio, and asserts the EXACT marker
# "M4: user/ring OK" appears on serial (the user/ring round-trip proof: the
# kernel drops to ring 3, the user stub issues `int 0x80`, the kernel handles it
# and returns). M0's hello, M1's trap round-trip, M2's ping-pong and M3's
# "M3: mmu OK" all print earlier in the same boot, so one run proves every
# milestone. Fail-closed: a wall-clock timeout bounds the run (the kernel halts
# rather than exiting), and a missing marker is a non-zero exit.
#
# PVH is selected automatically by QEMU from the XEN_ELFNOTE_PHYS32_ENTRY note
# in the ELF (see crates/tb-hal/src/arch/x86_64/boot.rs + kernel/linker/x86_64.ld).
#   refs: https://xenbits.xen.org/docs/unstable/misc/pvh.html
#         https://www.qemu.org/docs/master/system/i386/microvm.html
#
# Usage:   scripts/run-x86_64.sh [path/to/kernel-elf]
# Env:     QEMU=...  QEMU_TIMEOUT=<secs>  PROFILE=debug|release
set -euo pipefail

REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
TARGET="x86_64-tabos-none"
PROFILE="${PROFILE:-debug}"
KERNEL="${1:-${REPO_ROOT}/target/${TARGET}/${PROFILE}/tabos-kernel}"
MARKER='M4: user/ring OK'
TIMEOUT_SECS="${QEMU_TIMEOUT:-15}"
QEMU="${QEMU:-qemu-system-x86_64}"

if ! command -v "${QEMU}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "error: ${QEMU} not found on PATH (install qemu-system-x86 in WSL2)" >&2
  exit 2
fi
if [[ ! -f "${KERNEL}" ]]; then
  echo "error: kernel image not found: ${KERNEL}" >&2
  echo "build it first, e.g.:" >&2
  echo "  cargo build -p tabos-kernel --target ${REPO_ROOT}/targets/${TARGET}.json" >&2
  exit 2
fi

# Prefer KVM only when /dev/kvm is actually usable; otherwise pure-TCG so this
# runs in any WSL2 / CI box without nested virt.
ACCEL="tcg"
CPU="qemu64"
if [[ -e /dev/kvm && -r /dev/kvm && -w /dev/kvm ]]; then
  ACCEL="kvm"
  CPU="host"
fi

echo ">> qemu=${QEMU} accel=${ACCEL} cpu=${CPU} timeout=${TIMEOUT_SECS}s" >&2
echo ">> kernel=${KERNEL}" >&2

set +e
OUTPUT="$(timeout --foreground "${TIMEOUT_SECS}" \
  "${QEMU}" \
    -M microvm,rtc=off \
    -accel "${ACCEL}" -cpu "${CPU}" -m 256M -smp 1 \
    -kernel "${KERNEL}" \
    -no-reboot \
    -serial stdio -display none 2>&1)"
RC=$?
set -e

printf '%s\n' "${OUTPUT}"

if printf '%s' "${OUTPUT}" | grep -qF -- "${MARKER}"; then
  echo ">> PASS: observed marker '${MARKER}'" >&2
  exit 0
fi

echo ">> FAIL: marker '${MARKER}' not seen (qemu/timeout rc=${RC})" >&2
exit 1

files.note: OWNED. MARKER changed from 'M3: mmu OK' to 'M4: user/ring OK'. Header comments updated for M4; the QEMU virt launch (PL011, FDT in x0, -nographic serial) is unchanged.
files.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/run-aarch64.sh -- QEMU `virt` runner (milestone M4: user/ring boundary).
#
# Boots the aarch64 tb-os image under QEMU, captures the PL011 serial stream,
# and asserts the executable Definition-of-Done marker "M4: user/ring OK" (the
# EL0 round-trip proof: the kernel `eret`s to EL0, the user stub issues
# `svc #0`, the kernel handles it and returns). M0's hello, M1's trap
# round-trip, M2's ping-pong, "mmu-test: enabled, serial alive" and "M3: mmu OK"
# all print earlier in the same boot.
# Doubles as the cargo runner for target aarch64-tabos-none (cargo passes the
# freshly built ELF as $1) and is runnable by hand on WSL2.
#
# Invocation sources:
#   * QEMU `virt` board: UART0 = ARM PL011 @ 0x0900_0000, FDT pointer in x0,
#     `-kernel <ELF>` enters at EL1 (QEMU docs; dtb dumped via -machine dumpdtb).
#   * `-nographic` already routes the serial onto stdio AND muxes the monitor;
#     additionally passing `-serial stdio` makes QEMU abort with
#     "cannot use stdio by multiple character devices", so we deliberately do
#     not pass both -- `-nographic` *is* the requested serial-on-stdio path.
set -euo pipefail

REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
PROFILE="${PROFILE:-debug}"
KERNEL="${1:-${REPO_ROOT}/target/aarch64-tabos-none/${PROFILE}/tabos-kernel}"
QEMU="${QEMU_AARCH64:-qemu-system-aarch64}"
MARKER="M4: user/ring OK"
TIMEOUT_SECS="${QEMU_TIMEOUT:-15}"

if ! command -v "${QEMU}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "[run-aarch64] error: '${QEMU}' not found on PATH" >&2
    exit 127
fi

# -M virt          : QEMU AArch64 'virt' machine (PL011 @ 0x09000000, GICv2, FDT)
# -cpu cortex-a72  : real A72; guest entry = EL1h, MMU off, DAIF masked, x0=FDT
# -m 128M          : virt RAM = 0x4000_0000..0x4800_0000; image links at +512 KiB
# -nographic       : headless; PL011 -> this terminal's stdio (see note above)
# -no-reboot       : do not loop on a fatal guest event
# -kernel <ELF>    : load PT_LOADs at p_paddr, jump to e_entry (= _start)
set +e
OUTPUT="$(timeout --foreground "${TIMEOUT_SECS}" \
    "${QEMU}" \
        -M virt \
        -cpu cortex-a72 \
        -m 128M \
        -nographic \
        -no-reboot \
        -kernel "${KERNEL}" \
    < /dev/null 2>&1)"
QEMU_RC=$?
set -e

printf '%s\n' "${OUTPUT}"

if printf '%s' "${OUTPUT}" | grep -qF -- "${MARKER}"; then
    echo "[run-aarch64] PASS -- observed DoD marker: '${MARKER}'"
    exit 0
fi

echo "[run-aarch64] FAIL -- marker '${MARKER}' not seen" >&2
echo "[run-aarch64]   (qemu exit=${QEMU_RC}; the kernel halts in wfi, so a" >&2
echo "[run-aarch64]    ${TIMEOUT_SECS}s timeout/exit=124 is expected -- the grep is the verdict)" >&2
exit 1

reviews.verdict: x86_64 privilege boundary is CORRECT on every axis the lens asks for — no #GP/fault there. The iret frame is built [SS,RSP,RFLAGS,CS,RIP] with the args mapped right (SysV rdi=RIP,rsi=RSP,rdx=CS,rcx=SS; pushed rcx,rsi,rflags,rdx,rdi so RIP is on top); selectors are RPL=3 (USER_CODE_SEL|3=0x2B index5 DPL3, USER_DATA_SEL|3=0x33 index6 DPL3) and the GDT limit (56B, limit 55) covers index 6; TSS.RSP0 is programmed in gdt::init() (called by install_traps, which runs before user_demo) so the trap-back stack-switch is valid; the int-0x80 gate is DPL=3 (set_user -> type_attr 0xEE) so the software int from ring3 is permitted while still vectoring through KERNEL_CODE_SEL into ring0; all FOUR levels of the USER_VA walk set U/S=1 in a FRESH PML4[1]->PDPT[0]->PD[0]->PT[0/1] chain (PML4[0] correctly untouched), the code leaf is P|U with NX clear and no interior NX so ring3 can fetch, the stack leaf is P|RW|U|NX; the stub is genuinely position-independent (mov imm/int 0x80/jmp self, no RIP refs); syscall_entry re-aligns rsp (sub 8 after the CPU's 5 pushes) before the SysV call and does a correct non-local return via the parked KERNEL_RESUME_SP. RFLAGS=0x202 (IF settable because CPL=0<=IOPL=0 at iret), NT=0. I found NO privilege bug in the x86_64 code. However there are TWO real BLOCKERS that mean M4 cannot pass as it sits on disk: (1) the (correct) x86_64 round-trip is never invoked — it is orphaned; and (2) the entire aarch64 M4 half is unimplemented and its EL0 sync vector still routes to the fatal stub. The prompt's lib.rs/main.rs/aarch64 files show an intended state that is NOT on disk (verified by grep: user_demo/enter_ring3/set_user_gate appear ONLY in x86_64/{mod,user,idt}.rs).

issues.problem: The M4 round-trip is never wired through to the kernel, so the (correct) x86_64 ring3 code is dead and the DoD marker can never print. Verified on disk: the cfg dispatcher arch/mod.rs re-exports only {breakpoint,ctx_switch,halt,install_traps,mmu_init,mmu_selftest,serial_init,serial_write_byte,task_stack_init} for BOTH arches -- NOT user_demo. lib.rs defines no `pub fn user_demo` (grep: zero hits, no M4 section at all). kernel/src/main.rs ends at line 150 printing "M3: mmu OK" and never calls user_demo nor prints "M4: user/ring OK". Net effect: x86_64::user::user_demo exists and is privilege-correct but nothing reaches it; run-x86_64.sh greps for 'M4: user/ring OK', which is never emitted -> the runner times out and FAILs. (The prompt's lib.rs/main.rs that reference user_demo are not the on-disk files.)
issues.fix: Add `user_demo` to BOTH `pub use self::x86_64::{...}` and `pub use self::aarch64::{...}` in arch/mod.rs; add `pub fn user_demo() -> bool { arch::user_demo() }` to lib.rs (with an M4 doc section); in rust_main, after the M3 block, call it and print the marker, e.g. `if tb_hal::user_demo() { tb_hal::serial_write_str("M4: user/ring OK\n"); }`. Note: do NOT add the lib.rs facade until aarch64::user_demo exists (issue 2), or the aarch64 build will fail to resolve arch::user_demo.

issues.problem: The aarch64 M4 half does not exist on disk. There is no aarch64/user.rs (the prompt shows only a truncated module doc -- no enter_el0, no aarch64_el0_sync_handler, no user-page mapping to audit). aarch64/mod.rs does not declare `mod user` nor export user_demo. Critically, aarch64/vectors.rs still routes the 'Lower EL using AArch64, Synchronous' slot (index 8, byte offset 0x400) to `VEC_SLOT __vec_other`, the fatal stub -> aarch64_trap_handler(source=1) -> TrapKind::Other -> default hook returns Halt. So an EL0 `svc #0` would never reach an SVC handler; the core halts and the marker never prints (HANG/fail). Because the implementation is absent, none of the lens's EL0 privilege requirements can be confirmed: SPSR_EL1=EL0t on eret (doc claims 0x3C0, which IS correct: DAIF masked + M[4:0]=0), ESR_EL1.EC==0x15 check, SP_EL0 save, AP=0b01/AF=1, and especially UXN=0 on the code page -- the doc comment is cut off at PTE_PXN ('...so EL1 cannot ex') and never confirms UXN is CLEAR on the EL0-executable code leaf; a set UXN would instruction-abort (EC 0x20/0x21) on the first fetch, also unhandled -> hang.
issues.fix: Implement crates\tb-hal\src\arch\aarch64\user.rs (enter_el0 that programs SP_EL0/ELR_EL1/SPSR_EL1=0x3C0 then eret; aarch64_el0_sync_handler that checks ESR_EL1.EC==0x15 and longjmps back; an L1[3]->L2->L3 EL0 chain with the code leaf = PTE_TYPE_PAGE|PTE_USER(AP[1])|AF|SH, RDONLY(AP[2]) clear, UXN CLEAR, PXN set, AttrIndx=Normal). Add `mod user;` + `pub use user::user_demo;` to aarch64/mod.rs. In aarch64/vectors.rs change the 0x400 slot from `VEC_SLOT __vec_other` to `VEC_SLOT __vec_el0_sync` and add a `__vec_el0_sync:` trampoline (SAVE_CONTEXT; mov x1,#2; b into the EL0 handler). Until that exists the aarch64 DoD cannot pass.

reviews.verdict: BUILD BLOCKED by one concrete compile error. The x86_64 M4 round-trip itself (user.rs/gdt.rs/idt.rs) is correct: every privilege bit checks out (user code 0xFA -> selector 0x2B, user data 0xF2 -> 0x33, int-0x80 gate 0xEE with ring0 selector, TSS.RSP0 programmed + 16-aligned), the iretq frame push order [SS,RSP,RFLAGS,CS,RIP] is right, and the non-local-return stack math balances (rsp returns to user_demo at ==0 mod 16). M0/M1/M2/M3 surfaces in gdt.rs/idt.rs are preserved (GDT slots 0-4 untouched; vector 0x80 only flipped to DPL=3 inside user_demo, after M3), there are no name collisions, and the AtomicBool/AtomicU64/static-mut sharing and naked_asm!/global_asm! syntax all match the working M2 pattern. The single, fatal gap is wiring: user_demo is re-exported one layer too low and never reaches lib.rs.

issues.problem:
The cfg-dispatch arch/mod.rs does NOT re-export user_demo. lib.rs exposes the M4 surface as `pub fn user_demo() -> bool { arch::user_demo() }` (mirroring the mmu_init/mmu_selftest wrappers), but `arch::user_demo` resolves against THIS file, whose two re-export lists end at task_stack_init:
  `pub use self::x86_64::{breakpoint, ctx_switch, halt, install_traps, mmu_init, mmu_selftest, serial_init, serial_write_byte, task_stack_init};` (and the identical aarch64 list).
There is no glob re-export. arch/x86_64/mod.rs DOES add `pub use user::user_demo;` (and its comment claims it is 're-exported through arch/mod.rs so lib.rs can expose tb_hal::user_demo'), but that only makes the symbol reachable as `arch::x86_64::user_demo`, never as `arch::user_demo`. Result: lib.rs fails with E0425 'cannot find function `user_demo` in module `arch`' and the whole crate fails to build for BOTH targets. (Confirmed: arch/mod.rs on disk omits it and it is not in the M4 change set; the x86_64/mod.rs edit is necessary but not sufficient.)
issues.fix: Add `user_demo` to BOTH re-export lists in arch/mod.rs: change each `pub use self::x86_64::{... task_stack_init};` / `pub use self::aarch64::{... task_stack_init};` to also include `user_demo`. (Exactly how mmu_init/mmu_selftest were threaded through this file for M3.)

issues.problem: aarch64 M4 wiring is incomplete in the tree being built. On disk arch/aarch64/mod.rs is still the M3 version (no `mod user;`, no `pub use user::user_demo;`), and arch/aarch64/user.rs does not exist (Read returns 'cannot find the file'). The proposed aarch64/vectors.rs routes the Lower-EL Sync slot to `extern aarch64_el0_sync_handler`, which is defined in user.rs. If the aarch64 arm is built (scripts/run-aarch64.sh) without the proposed aarch64/mod.rs (declaring `mod user;` + re-export) AND a real aarch64/user.rs defining both `aarch64_el0_sync_handler` and `user_demo`, the aarch64 target fails to compile/link (unresolved `aarch64_el0_sync_handler`, missing `mod user`). This is the same class of gap as the arch/mod.rs blocker: the per-arch wiring was not applied consistently.
issues.fix: Land the proposed aarch64/mod.rs (with `mod user;` + `pub use user::user_demo;`) and the full aarch64/user.rs (defining `aarch64_el0_sync_handler` with the signature vectors.rs expects, plus `user_demo`), then ensure arch/mod.rs's aarch64 `pub use` list also carries `user_demo` (covered by the blocker fix). Verify the aarch64 build, not just x86_64.

issues.problem: The kernel main.rs on disk is still the M3 shim: it ends `if tb_hal::mmu_selftest() { ... 'M3: mmu OK' ... } tb_hal::halt()` and never calls user_demo, so even if the crate compiled, the DoD marker 'M4: user/ring OK' that scripts/run-x86_64.sh greps for would never print and the run would fail-closed. The prompt's FILES show an M4-titled main.rs, so this is presumably part of the change, but as the tree stands the M4 call site is absent. M1 trap + M2 ping-pong + M3 mmu ordering is otherwise preserved verbatim.
issues.fix: Insert the M4 step after the M3 block and before halt(): `if tb_hal::user_demo() { tb_hal::serial_write_str("M4: user/ring OK\n"); } else { tb_hal::serial_write_str("M4: FAIL\n"); }`, keeping the existing M0->M1->M2->M3 sequence ahead of it.

issues.problem: The M4 round-trip is never wired through to the kernel, so the (correct) x86_64 ring3 code is dead and the DoD marker can never print. Verified on disk: the cfg dispatcher arch/mod.rs re-exports only {breakpoint,ctx_switch,halt,install_traps,mmu_init,mmu_selftest,serial_init,serial_write_byte,task_stack_init} for BOTH arches -- NOT user_demo. lib.rs defines no `pub fn user_demo` (grep: zero hits, no M4 section at all). kernel/src/main.rs ends at line 150 printing "M3: mmu OK" and never calls user_demo nor prints "M4: user/ring OK". Net effect: x86_64::user::user_demo exists and is privilege-correct but nothing reaches it; run-x86_64.sh greps for 'M4: user/ring OK', which is never emitted -> the runner times out and FAILs. (The prompt's lib.rs/main.rs that reference user_demo are not the on-disk files.)
issues.fix: Add `user_demo` to BOTH `pub use self::x86_64::{...}` and `pub use self::aarch64::{...}` in arch/mod.rs; add `pub fn user_demo() -> bool { arch::user_demo() }` to lib.rs (with an M4 doc section); in rust_main, after the M3 block, call it and print the marker, e.g. `if tb_hal::user_demo() { tb_hal::serial_write_str("M4: user/ring OK\n"); }`. Note: do NOT add the lib.rs facade until aarch64::user_demo exists (issue 2), or the aarch64 build will fail to resolve arch::user_demo.

issues.problem: The aarch64 M4 half does not exist on disk. There is no aarch64/user.rs (the prompt shows only a truncated module doc -- no enter_el0, no aarch64_el0_sync_handler, no user-page mapping to audit). aarch64/mod.rs does not declare `mod user` nor export user_demo. Critically, aarch64/vectors.rs still routes the 'Lower EL using AArch64, Synchronous' slot (index 8, byte offset 0x400) to `VEC_SLOT __vec_other`, the fatal stub -> aarch64_trap_handler(source=1) -> TrapKind::Other -> default hook returns Halt. So an EL0 `svc #0` would never reach an SVC handler; the core halts and the marker never prints (HANG/fail). Because the implementation is absent, none of the lens's EL0 privilege requirements can be confirmed: SPSR_EL1=EL0t on eret (doc claims 0x3C0, which IS correct: DAIF masked + M[4:0]=0), ESR_EL1.EC==0x15 check, SP_EL0 save, AP=0b01/AF=1, and especially UXN=0 on the code page -- the doc comment is cut off at PTE_PXN ('...so EL1 cannot ex') and never confirms UXN is CLEAR on the EL0-executable code leaf; a set UXN would instruction-abort (EC 0x20/0x21) on the first fetch, also unhandled -> hang.
issues.fix: Implement crates\tb-hal\src\arch\aarch64\user.rs (enter_el0 that programs SP_EL0/ELR_EL1/SPSR_EL1=0x3C0 then eret; aarch64_el0_sync_handler that checks ESR_EL1.EC==0x15 and longjmps back; an L1[3]->L2->L3 EL0 chain with the code leaf = PTE_TYPE_PAGE|PTE_USER(AP[1])|AF|SH, RDONLY(AP[2]) clear, UXN CLEAR, PXN set, AttrIndx=Normal). Add `mod user;` + `pub use user::user_demo;` to aarch64/mod.rs. In aarch64/vectors.rs change the 0x400 slot from `VEC_SLOT __vec_other` to `VEC_SLOT __vec_el0_sync` and add a `__vec_el0_sync:` trampoline (SAVE_CONTEXT; mov x1,#2; b into the EL0 handler). Until that exists the aarch64 DoD cannot pass.

issues.problem:
The cfg-dispatch arch/mod.rs does NOT re-export user_demo. lib.rs exposes the M4 surface as `pub fn user_demo() -> bool { arch::user_demo() }` (mirroring the mmu_init/mmu_selftest wrappers), but `arch::user_demo` resolves against THIS file, whose two re-export lists end at task_stack_init:
  `pub use self::x86_64::{breakpoint, ctx_switch, halt, install_traps, mmu_init, mmu_selftest, serial_init, serial_write_byte, task_stack_init};` (and the identical aarch64 list).
There is no glob re-export. arch/x86_64/mod.rs DOES add `pub use user::user_demo;` (and its comment claims it is 're-exported through arch/mod.rs so lib.rs can expose tb_hal::user_demo'), but that only makes the symbol reachable as `arch::x86_64::user_demo`, never as `arch::user_demo`. Result: lib.rs fails with E0425 'cannot find function `user_demo` in module `arch`' and the whole crate fails to build for BOTH targets. (Confirmed: arch/mod.rs on disk omits it and it is not in the M4 change set; the x86_64/mod.rs edit is necessary but not sufficient.)
issues.fix: Add `user_demo` to BOTH re-export lists in arch/mod.rs: change each `pub use self::x86_64::{... task_stack_init};` / `pub use self::aarch64::{... task_stack_init};` to also include `user_demo`. (Exactly how mmu_init/mmu_selftest were threaded through this file for M3.)

issues.problem: aarch64 M4 wiring is incomplete in the tree being built. On disk arch/aarch64/mod.rs is still the M3 version (no `mod user;`, no `pub use user::user_demo;`), and arch/aarch64/user.rs does not exist (Read returns 'cannot find the file'). The proposed aarch64/vectors.rs routes the Lower-EL Sync slot to `extern aarch64_el0_sync_handler`, which is defined in user.rs. If the aarch64 arm is built (scripts/run-aarch64.sh) without the proposed aarch64/mod.rs (declaring `mod user;` + re-export) AND a real aarch64/user.rs defining both `aarch64_el0_sync_handler` and `user_demo`, the aarch64 target fails to compile/link (unresolved `aarch64_el0_sync_handler`, missing `mod user`). This is the same class of gap as the arch/mod.rs blocker: the per-arch wiring was not applied consistently.
issues.fix: Land the proposed aarch64/mod.rs (with `mod user;` + `pub use user::user_demo;`) and the full aarch64/user.rs (defining `aarch64_el0_sync_handler` with the signature vectors.rs expects, plus `user_demo`), then ensure arch/mod.rs's aarch64 `pub use` list also carries `user_demo` (covered by the blocker fix). Verify the aarch64 build, not just x86_64.

issues.problem: The kernel main.rs on disk is still the M3 shim: it ends `if tb_hal::mmu_selftest() { ... 'M3: mmu OK' ... } tb_hal::halt()` and never calls user_demo, so even if the crate compiled, the DoD marker 'M4: user/ring OK' that scripts/run-x86_64.sh greps for would never print and the run would fail-closed. The prompt's FILES show an M4-titled main.rs, so this is presumably part of the change, but as the tree stands the M4 call site is absent. M1 trap + M2 ping-pong + M3 mmu ordering is otherwise preserved verbatim.
issues.fix: Insert the M4 step after the M3 block and before halt(): `if tb_hal::user_demo() { tb_hal::serial_write_str("M4: user/ring OK\n"); } else { tb_hal::serial_write_str("M4: FAIL\n"); }`, keeping the existing M0->M1->M2->M3 sequence ahead of it.
